segment-text: 1. INT. AIRPORT CAFETERIA - DAY

A cafeteria in a quiet airport in the provinces. Fe w passers-by. A single woman in her mid-40s (JULIETTE) sits at a table. Sips coffee. Gazes around her as if discovering something new - she often has an expression of eager surprise, as if seeing things, people, places for the first time ever. She seems uncomfortable in the clothes she is wearing. At her feet is an old-fashioned suitcase. A younger woman (LEA, early 30s) appears behind Juliette. Less than a meter away. Juliette hasn't noticed her. Clearly deeply moved, Lea quietly observes her for a few seconds. Then, sensing a presence, Juliette looks round. Lea leans forward and hugs her tight before Juliette has time to react. Juliette is slightly taken aback. She doesn't return the hug at first. Then awkwardly puts her arms round Lea.
2. INT. LEA'S CAR - DAY
Lea is driving. Next to her, Juliette tugs at the sleeves of her sweater.
Is it too big?
No.
I like that sweater. The skirt, too. I often wear them.
Juliette doesn't reply.
I thought it would be better to wait until you arrived to go shopping for clothes.
Lea glances at Juliette.
There's something out of place about Juliette. She even sits oddly in the car seat, as if unused to it.
Did you have a good trip?
I don't like flying.
Lea glances at her again.
Have you lived here long?
Ten years. I finished my PhD here, met Luc, got married, was offered the job... The usual stuff.
Juliette is silent. The CAMERA lingers on her face.
3. INT. LEA'S HOUSE - DAY
Lea shows Juliette into the house.
Welcome to our kingdom! The kitchen's over there with the living room opposite, our home offices and a library at the far end. Upstairs, there's just bedrooms. Do you want to see yours?
Juliette replies by a slight nod and the flicker of a smile. Lea leads the way up the stairs. On the first landing, she shows Juliette into a room.
Will it be okay?
Fine.
That little door leads to your private bathroom. (glances at her watch) Yikes, I have to pick up the girls! I'll be back in half an hour. Will you be okay on your own?
Juliette nods. Lea hurries out. On the landing, she stops in her tracks. Slowly retraces her step, looks at Juliette and says in a serious tone:
I'm glad you're here.
Lea gives Juliette a kiss on the cheek and goes. Juliette is expressionless. OS, feet dashing down the stairs. The jangle of keys.
Take a look round the house, if you want!
The front door thunks closed. Juliette hasn't moved an inch. She remains motionless like that for a long Moment.
4. INT. LEA'S HOUSE - DAY MONTAGE of Juliette's tour of the house.

In the living room, she strokes a cat dozing on a chair. In a study, she runs her hand over the back of a couch. Gazes at framed photos. In the kitchen, she pours herself a glass of water. Drinks slowly, leaning back against the fridge. Upstairs, Juliette opens a door. A child's bedroom. She closes the door hurriedly. She opens another door. A library full of books. With a single bed and two chairs. Sitting in one of the chairs, an elderly man is reading.
I'm sorry, I... I didn't think anyone was home.
The old man looks up without a word and smiles at her.
I... My name's Juliette. I'm Lea's sister.
Still not a word. Just a kindly smile.
Like I said, I'm very sorry.
Smiling, the old man goes back to his book, as if indifferent to her presence.
5. EXT/INT. LEA'S HOUSE - DAY

Lea closes the back door of the car. She has a two -year-old girl (EMELIA) in her arms. Another little girl, (CLELIS, known as Little Lili) waits on the sidewalk. She looks 8-9 years old. Both girls are Asian.
Lili, open the gate, will you? You know it's hard when I'm carrying Emelia.
They go through the gate into the small front garden.
Is Auntie Juliette going to stay with us forever?
No, sweetheart, just some time. Be nice to her, okay? She's tired. Don't
pester her. They enter the house.
Juliette? It's us!
Juliette comes down the stairs. The kids gaze up at her. As soon as she reaches the bottom step, Lili rushes over.
Hello, Auntie!

Juliette seems quite distant, or maybe just ill at ease. Lili stands on tiptoes to give her a kiss. Juliette hesitates fractionally before giving her a kiss back. Emelia gurgles the word "Auntie" repeatedly.
These are my two princesses, Clelis - but everybody calls her Little Lili - and little miss Emelia. Give Juliette a nice smile, sweety!
Auntie, come and see my bedroom!
Don't start! Go straight into the kitchen. There's milk and biscuits on
the table. Chop, chop. The two girls run off into the kitchen .
Did you look round the house?
Yes, I... I went into the library. There was an old man...
Of course, silly me, I should have warned you. That's Luc's father.
(yells) Papy Paul! His brain's gone all mushy
and he lost his tongue!
Lili! He had a bad stroke three years ago. He hasn't spoken a word since. He
spends all day reading. You'll see, he's a lovely man.
6. INT. KITCHEN - EVENING

Dinner time. The whole family is round the table: Lea, the two girls, Juliette, Papy Paul - smiling dumbly - and Luc, Lea's husband, also in his early-mid 30s. The two young girls are very excited. Lea makes a big effort to seem relaxed. Juliette still seems a little strange, as if everything is new to her. And Luc just seems a bit uptight.
Have you ever been to the Lorraine region before?
No, this is the first time.
Everybody thinks it's just like being in Germany, that it's freezing cold all the time...
It is!
Not all the time.
Just eat your dinner!
Were you born here?
Yes, but my Dad comes from Poland. He arrived after the war. Isn't that right, Dad?
The old man merely smiles a little more insistently than usual.
Luc's Mum was Russian. He married a girl who's half-French, half-English. And our two daughters are Viet. It's all the colors of Benetton in this house.
You've lost your accent, Mummy, but Auntie Juliette still has one.
Well, she grew up in England.
Anybody see the water jug?

Katrina broke it and I haven't had time to buy another. (to Juliette) Katrina's the cleaner. She comes every Thursday. Hide anything fragile. She's always breaking things. That's why we call her Katrina. Because she leaves a trail of devastation behind her! Her real name's Marie-Paule.
(to Luc) Lea said you do research at the university?
(jokily) You two sound like we're at a party full of strangers.
Daddy's a lexicolographer.
Lexicographer's good enough for me. Our unit is working on an interactive, intuitive dictionary that can b e reactivated in real time.
It's you guys that need to reactivate. In seven years, they've only got to 'C'. Even the Academie Francaise goes faster than that.
There's no comparison. That reminds me, there was a message on the machine from one of your students, a guy called Bamake or Bakame, something like that. A problem with his marks, I think.
Bamakale? I don't believe it! He won't give up.
Why didn't we ever meet you before, Auntie?
Everyone falls silent. Juliette doesn't know what to say. Luc glances anxiously at Lea. The two girls don't seem to notice. Papy Paul keeps on eating, smiling away.
I was... away. For a very long time.
Lea changes the subject. A little too quickly. She hands Juliette a dish.
Do you want some more?
No, thanks.
Don't you like it?
It's very good, but...
Where were you away?
(snaps) Is this some kind of interrogation! Leave your aunt in peace! Eat!
7. INT. LEA & LUC'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Lea and Luc are ready for bed.
How long does she plan on staying?
How should I know? As long as she needs. A few weeks, a few months... Is it a problem?
To be honest, yes. Okay, she's your sister, but...
But what?
You heard Lili. She wanted to know.
So?
Her being "away" won't work for long.
It'll work as long as it works, we'll see.
"We'll see! We'll see!" You make me
laugh. What are you going to say to
Luc, don't give me a hard time. This is our first evening. I've just got my sister back. I don't know if you realize what that means. I've just got
her back.
You hardly know her. Just because you went to see her a couple of times for a few hours in the last few months doesn't... You were only a teenager when she -
Be quiet!
Okay, I'll be quiet. Quiet as a mouse. (beat) But think of us a little, too.
8. INT. JULIETTE'S BEDR OOM - NIGHT

Juliette stands by the window. We can vaguely make out the garden below. Juliette is still wearing Lea's clothes Lea. She goes over to the bed. Sits on the edge, as if she plans to spend the whole night like that.
9. INT. KITCHEN - DAY

It's total, happy, bubbly chaos in the kitchen. The girls are finishing their breakfast. Lea is a whirl of fevered activity. Still in his pyjamas, Papy Paul slurps coffee from a bowl, smiling as always. Juliette observes the scene, leaning against the wall with a mug of tea in one hand.
C'mon, kids! Chop, chop, we're gonna be late!
Aren't we always?
Emelia, what are you doing? What are you doing? I don't believe it!
Emelia has smeared yoghurt all over her dress.
I'll have to change your dress now. Lili, finish up quickly.
Lea dashes out with Emelia in her arms. Papy Paul leaves also. Juliette and Little Lili are alone.
Don't you eat breakfast?
It depends.
I'm never hungry in the morning. Ever since I was a little girl. Mummy gets so angry! Did you ever see me when I was little, when Mummy and Daddy brought me back from Vietnam?
Sure.
I don't believe you. Your nose is getting longer. (beat) Daddy!
Luc has just entered. Little Lili throws her arms round him.
(awkwardly) Morning, Juliette. Did you sleep well?
EXT. SCHOOL GATES - DAY
Lea and Little Lili get out of the car and join the flow of mothers and children. Lea turns and leans towards the passenger side window.
You're not coming?
I'll wait here.
'Bye, Auntie!
Juliette looks overwhelmed by it all. She doesn't answer. Little Lili and Lea walk toward the gates.
I put some more canteen tickets in your bag.
I noticed.
Your gym stuff is in there, too. Try not to lose anything this time.
Mummy?
What?
Auntie's a bit weird, isn't she?
What makes you say that?
I don't know. She doesn't say much.
Give her time to adapt, eh? Go on, go!
A kiss and Little Lili walks into school.
Ask Madame Rouyer if she found your pink beret!
INT. LEA'S CAR - DAY
Juliette is in the passenger seat. Lea's face looms up at the window. Juliette stares straight ahead, breath ing heavily. Lea walks round and gets in behind the wheel.
Are you okay?
Juliette nods several times, her breath rasping slightly.
Sure?
Yes.
Lea pulls out and drives in silence for a moment.
The police station is close to the university. I'm teaching for two hours, but we can meet up after, if you want? There's a nice cafe just
opposite the campus gates.
Okay.
You know what they want to see you for?
I have to sign in at regular times. Beat.
Didn't you ever see anyone? I mean, did anyone come to see you?
The visitors.
(beat) Do you hate me?
What for?
For never coming to see you.
Juliette shrugs.
I wrote at first.
I never received any letters.
One evening, Dad saw me. Mummy and him gave me a real roasting. They banned me from writing. They said you... you didn't exist. I was a kid then you know.
I meant to tell you...
What?
It was the social services who came up with the idea to call you just before I got out, not me.
I'm glad they did. Beat.
I'm glad they did.
INT. WAITING ROOM, POLICE STATION - DAY
Juliette sits on a bench with an OLD LADY beside her. The old lady glances at Juliette. Smiles. Juliette smiles back. Beat. The old lady glances at her again.
Are you reporting a crime, too?
No.
Just then, an INSPECTOR appears. Plain clothes, same age as Juliette.
Juliette Fontaine?
Yes.
Follow me, please.
INT. INSPECTOR'S OFFICE - DAY

Juliette sits opposite the Inspector. He flicks through a file, pores over a couple of pages, goes back a few pages and re-reads, frowning, but not hostile or judgmental - friendly almost. He closes the file.
I see you're a doctor.
Was. I was struck off the medical register.

Makes no difference. Once a doctor, always a doctor. It's like me. I'll always be a cop. (suddenly) You have a pretty name. I don't like my name, but Fontaine is very pretty. It brings to mind gurgling water, streams, rivers... The Orinoco. You know the Orinoco? No?
He uses his pen to point to a poster on the wall behind him, which shows a broad swathe of an unidentifiable river.

There'll be no more fountains soon. Municipalities are closing them down - even in tiny villages. They're filled with earth and made into flower beds. Stupid geraniums, usually. (switches topic again) I won't hassle you. The law says we have to see each other, so we see each other, but I won't give you any bother. Do you have a job?
Not yet.
A place to stay.
With my sister.
That's nice. Family's important. You're lucky. I live alone. Divorced. Small apartment. Small bed. Anyway, you don't want to hear all that.
He gets up, goes to the door.
See you in two weeks?
Goodbye.
Goodbye.
INT/EXT. UNIVERSITY CAMPUS - DAY

Lea walks down a hallway. Groups of students hang out, shooting the breeze. Lea nods to a colleague going the other way. She enters the staff room, checks through the mail in her box, throws some stuff away. She opens and scans one letter. Then another that makes her roll her eyes.
Not him again! He's a real pain!
Another colleague (MICHEL) approaches and whispers into Lea's ear.
One of your many lovers writing to you?
Lea doesn't look round, just keeps reading.
If only! Bamakale!
Ah ha! Prosper Napoleon Bamakale, descendant of ancient Togolese royalty, son of a prince of royal blood, repeating his first year for the third time.
I'm getting fond of him, actually. I keep telling him it's not a mistake, but he doesn't get it.
Tell me about it! I had him all last year. Are you off?
I'm done for the day.
I'll walk you to your car.
They step outside without a break in their conver sation.
Have you finished marking your mid terms?
Almost. I have ten or so left to do.
Lucky you! I haven't even started.
Every time I look at the pile on my desk, I feel so exhausted.
Get a move on though, the Dean wants all the marks in by the 18 .
The Dean! (changes subject) What did you decide for that conference I told you about?
We'll see.
Lea sees Juliette waiting by the car.
You're already here? I thought we were meeting in the cafe.
It didn't take very long.
Michel, this is Juliette, my sister.
Juliette, this is Michel, a colleague.
Hello... (to Lea) You never told me you had a sister, and a very pretty one at that.
Don't listen to him. Michel's a specialist on love letters from the 16th century to the present day. And it rubs off on him. (to Michel) Friday of next week, are you free?
Why?
Dinner at our house, with Samir and Kaisha.
I should be able to make it. Can I let you know next week?
Sure. Don't forget your marking.
Goodbye, Juliette.
Goodbye.
C'mon, let's go shopping.
INT. TEA ROOMS - DAY
Juliette and Lea are sitting at a table with a cup of tea each and a plate of cakes. On the chair next to them and at their feet are shopping bags f rom various stores.
Good?
(nods) You remember Bouchard's in Rouen?
No.
It was a bit like this. I used to take you there on a Wednesday after your ballet lesson.
With Old Mother Stabush?
Fat Old Mother Stabush!
She couldn't even do points anymore. I remember she had a moustache, too. We were all scared of her.
I was at uni and I always picked you up and took you to Bouchard's. You had a cream puff every time.
(tremulously) I don't remember.
You were only seven or eight. There were always stacks of old ladies in there...
(like it's a real drama) Why don't I remember?
You sat there so proud. A proper little lady with your cream cake.
(tears up) I don't even remember it.
Juliette suddenly realizes that Lea is distraught.
Don't worry, you were only little.
INT. LITTLE LILI'S BEDROOM - EVENING
The door opens. Little Lili and Juliette enter the bedroom, followed by Emelia.
(to Emelia) No, you're not coming in! Out! It's my room! You can show her yours later. (closes the door) Kid sisters are so annoying! Was Mummy
like that when she was little?
She wasn't too bad.
Is she your real sister?
Yes.
You're Granny Lizbeth's daughter, too?
That's right.
Okay, this is the doll graveyard.
She opens a suitcase filled with a dozen dolls.
I don't like dolls, so when someone gives me one, I bury it in here right
away. That's my computer. I watch DVDs
Dinner time!
Do you read a lot?
Juliette runs her fingers along the spines of the books. Lingers over one she apparently remembers from her childhood, called Model Little Ladies.
Less than Papy Paul, but quite a lot, yes. This is my secret diary - well, it's not so secret seeing as I leave it out and everybody can read it. I write poems in it. Do you want me to read you one?
(recoils) No.
Please, Auntie! Just a little one.
(snaps) No. I said no!
Little Lili looks at her in amazement. Juliette scurries out of the room.
INT. LIVING ROOM - DAY
Juliette is with a plump, smiling young woman ( the SOCIAL WORKER). They each sit on a couch facing each other across a coffee table.
You're lucky to have your sister, her
family and a home.
So I've been told.
It's true, though. Most people, when they get out, don't know where to go. Nobody wants them. Their spouses almost always file for divorce.
I got that out of the way before I went in.
Yes, it's in your file. Right, well, the courses you did at Centrale will be very useful. People always n eed secretaries. Are your IT skills up to speed?
I did all the updates whenever I got the chance.

That's good. I got you an interview at this company... (hands Juliette a card) They're looking for someone. You have an appointment with a Monsieur Dupuis. We'll be meeting regularly, but if you have any questions or anything, don't hesitate to call me at the office...
Right.
I have to go now. You have people you can talk to, and there's only two of us in the department. (beat)
I like the house.
They get up. Juliette walks her to the door. They say goodbye. Outside, Lea parks her car and gets out. She meets the Social Worker by the gate. They nod. Lea enters.
Was that the social worker?
Yes.
She seems sweet enough.
People whose role is to rummage through your life often seem sweet enough.
Lea glances curiously at her sister. Puts her bag down. Thinks...

I guess you're right. We had that, too - Luc and me - when we adopted. Visits for this, visits for that, social workers, shrinks, and even an idiot doctor who had Luc walking up and down in his underpants in his office with his arms stretched out! I thought Luc was going to strangle him. He really resented all the questions and prying... Tea?
INT. KITCHEN - DAY
Lea is making tea. Juliette stands close by.

It took nearly two years for Little Lili and a bit less for Emelia. The first time we spent three months in Vietnam. We fell in love with the place and would have happily stayed longer. We had Little Lili with us almost the whole time, at the hotel, but the bureaucracy and all the formalities took forever.
KATRINA (O.S.)
Yes, Marie-Paule?
Shall I do the crystal, too?
No, don't worry, leave it! I'll do it! Do the cupboard doors, will you?
How old was Little Lili?
Two. But she looked half that.
You met her mother?

No. But I have her address if ever she wants to contact her. That was very important for us. We didn't want her to run into a brick wall later if she tried to find her. Emelia, on the other hand... Well, we got absolutely no information. She'll never know...
How old was she?
She was tiny! Barely three weeks old. Beat.
Was it Luc or you who couldn't have kids?
Neither of us. We're both fertile. (beat) I didn't want a child of my own.
Because of me? No answer.
Because of what I did? Beat.
I never tried to work out why.
INT. DUPUIS' OFFICE - DAY
MONSIEUR DUPUIS, early 50s, smug, sits behind his desk, talking on the phone. Juliette sits opposite him.
Yes, well... So what?... That's not my problem. We pay you to deliver our order, so you deliver it, period! Goodbye!
He hangs up. Gazes at Juliette.
Right, where were we... I won't beat around the bush. I'm not a charity. I want skilled, hard-working staff. The rest is not my problem. Mrs... what's her name again? Balboukian?
That's right.
... said that you are up to speed with Excel, DTP and the whole shebang. Right?
I passed all the tests.
And you speak English?
And Spanish.
I don't give a shit about Spanish but we have a lot of clients across the Channel. There's stacks of stuff that needs translating... How long were you in jail?
Fifteen years.
(whistles) Jeez! You knock off the President or something? No answer.
What did you do? Still no answer.
I want to know. What did you do to get so long? Kill someone? Did you?
Yes.
Who? Your husband? Lover? Another woman? No answer.
I'm talking to you. Who did you kill?
My son. My six-year-old son.
The blood seeps out of Dupuis' face. He drops the pencil he was fiddling with and pushes back in his chair.
He gets slowly to his feet.
(rasps) Get out! Get the hell out of my office!
INT. CAFE - DAY

Juliette is in a small bar, near the window, watching events outside and in. At the bar a GUY, who's good-looking and knows it, talks animatedly with another guy and the waitress. Nothing of their conversation filters over, except the occasional burst of laughter. Juliette glances over a couple of times, when they laugh particularly loudly. Eventually, the guy notices Juliette, and watches her while continuing his conversation with the others. After a while, he comes over. Without asking, he takes a seat at Juliette's table.
You want my photo?
Juliette doesn't answer.
Have we met?
I don't think so.
Why were you staring at me then? What do you want?
Nothing. I wasn't staring at you.
Bullshit! You keep eying me up. You looking for a man?
Juliette smiles, amused by his question. Then, she eyes him up and down.
INT. HOTEL ROOM - DAY
Juliette lies pensively in bed, the sheets pulled up to her chin. The guy comes out of the bathroom and finishes dressing.
Was it good?
Juliette gazes at him before answering.
No. Not at all. But it's okay.
The guy looks furious. He mumbles something, grabs his jacket and storms out. The camera holds on Juliette, who hasn't moved an inch.
EXT. PARK - DAY
Lea and Juliette walk side by side, chatting. The two little girls run ahead. Wide shot. Then the CAMERA is right behind them. The children are already standing at some animal cages.
Auntie, look! They're monkeys. Sometimes they spit at people.
They all stop by the monkey cages. Little Lili and Emelia pulls faces.
It's not fair locking them up. They haven't done anything. Prisons are for bad people, not animals? Isn't that so, Mummy? Eh, Auntie?
Lea grabs Little Lili by the shoulder.
We're going!
What did I do?
Nothing, get your sister. We'll go and buy you some waffles.
The two girls race ahead once more.
Don't be silly.
Lea doesn't answer. They walk along in silence.

Do you think you can erase fifteen years of your life like that? Just by not talking about it? Do you think I was asleep the whole time, like in some kind of fairy tale? Then, one morning, the good fairy Lea came to wake me up?
INT. LEA & LUC'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Lea is in bed, reading a book. Lea sits on the edge of the bed, listening to the soccer results on a transistor radio. RADIO ANNOUNCER (O.C.)
Yes! PSG lost! Ha ha! They can stick it up their asses!
He flicks off his radio.
What are you doing?
Can't you see? I'm peeling potatoes.
Very funny. Jeez, 3-1! (rubs his hands together)
Michel's gonna be in such a bad mood. Luc lies back and gazes at the ceiling.
Are the chicklets asleep?
After all the running they did, they were flat out at seven.
What about your sister?
What about her? Is she asleep, do you mean?
No... What did... I don't know... Do you talk?
Yes. We talk.
Did you ask her...
What?
You know... Why? Why she did it?
(wide-eyed) What the hell is wrong with you?
Well, what do you talk about then?
Sister things.
You're shitting me!

What do you expect me to say? It all takes time. It's not easy. My parents banished any thought of my sister from my head. Now, I've got her back. It's like a re-birth. What happened before is... Oh, just let me sleep!
She puts her book down, turns off her light and rolls over.
INT. DINING ROOM - EVENING

Desserts has been served. Around the table are Lea, Luc, Papy Paul, Michel, Juliette and another couple - Samir and his younger, pregnant wife, Kaisha . Through the partition doors, we see Little Lili and Emelia asleep on the couches, fully dressed. At table, the atmosphere is upbeat with all the guests laughing and joking happily. Sitting next to Michel, Juliette seems quite relaxed, too, but still a little reserved. Just like Luc, who occasionally glances apprehensively at her. The conversation opens O.S., the time it takes for the camera to track past the two kids into the dining room.
And you know your father backs me 100% on this one. Don't you, Papy Paul?
You must be joking! Dad's the most loyal supporter Nancy have ever had. Aren't you, Dad?
He reaches for the salad bowl.
Watch out! I glued it back together but I'm not promising anything.
Katrina?
Lea nods.
Michel, it's outrageous that you've lived in Lorraine for years and you still support a team that's mostly made up of Brazilians.
Mostly? Only two Brazilians play for PSG! You don't know what you're talking about! Anyway, you'll bore Juliette!
Unlikely! I love football. I played a lot when I was a teenager.
Seriously?
You were too small to remember.
The perfect woman! Beautiful, intelligent and loves football.
Except for the supporters.
Take that!
Please, Samir, I won't let an Afghan surgeon paid by al-Qaida to run amok in French hospitals beat me in an argument about football.
Can you play on Sunday?
Forget it. He was saying earlier that he has a zillion exams to mark.
That's the third week running you've bailed on us.
Yeah, it's been three weeks that two hundred papers have been sitting on my desk, waiting for me to mark them.
You play football together?
The camera closes in on Michel and Juliette. OS, the buzz of the others' conversation.
We try. Some days, there's only ten of us and some days forty people turn up to play, which gets a bit crazy. You should come watch us one day.
Why not.
Do you like it here?
Juliette nods.
Lea said you lived down south for a long time.
That's right.
Where?
Right, it's pretty down there. The Black Mountain and all that. Toulouse not far away...
Do you come from Paris?
Yes, but I got bored of it. I go back as infrequently as possible. Life's good here. If you can put up with lunkheads like these guys!
They smile at each other.
INT. LIBRARY, LEA AND LUC'S HOUSE - DAY
Juliette quietly enters Papy Paul's room. He sits in his usual chair, reading. He looks up and smiles at Juliette.
Can I sit with you a while?
She sits in the chair opposite him. Papy Paul goes back to his book. Beat. Juliette begins to talk. Papy Paul smiles at her.
Where I was until recently, I always kept a few books near my pillow. I sensed their presence at night when I turned over. It reassured me.
She leans forward to see the title of the book he's reading: Sylvie by Nerval.
I read and re-read that one. Not so much as a means of escape but like a dream.
INT. INSPECTOR'S OFFICE - DAY
Juliette is sitting opposite the Inspector as in the previous scene.

During the week, I eat canned food mostly - lentils and sausages or beef stew. Those are my favourites. I eat in front of the TV onSometimes, I don't even heat them up. I can't cook to save my life and I can't be bothered to learn. I have some green vegetables at the weekend. Doctor's orders. Do you agree with him?
Definitely. You can't just eat tinned food.
I guess... What about eggs?
They're okay, but not every day.
No, I usually boil or fry one up on a Sunday and eat it in front of the TV. Do you watch TV?
No.
Not even when you were in prison?
No.

I see. I thought everybody in prison watched TV. I force myself. It's a kind of punishment. There's something totally ugly about it. Like a pile of rubbish that's delivered to your door. Every evening, I feel like the heap's bigger and uglier than the day before. And it smacks me in the face. (beat) How about you? Everything okay?
INT. FACULTY BUILDINGS - DAY

Juliette waits in a hallway. A door opens and students file out. Juliette ventures in. Lea stands on a raised dais, gathering her things together, while talking to a black student dressed in suit and tie.
We'll check as soon as we can, Mr. Bamakale, but I can't see why the computer would have made a mistake.
It made two last year alone, Miss. And every time, I'm the aggrieved party.
In any case, the server is down right now, so be patient and stop sending me notes every other day. (sees Juliette) You'll have to excuse me, I have an appointment.
Lea goes over to Juliette.
Am I glad to see you! Let's get out of here. I thought you were going straight home.
I wanted to talk.
INT. CAFE - DAY
Juliette and Lea sit at a table.
It was a minor intestinal haemorrhage, but they kept him in for tests. He was riddled with cancer. He died two months later.
When exactly?

March 4, 1999. When we found out he had no chance of pulling through, I wanted to tell you. I had a huge fight with Mummy, and she went and told him. He made me promise never to contact you. He was crying. His voice came out in a feeble rasp. He was hooked up to dozens of tubes. He weighed barely more than fifty kilos. I cried, too. And I promised him. I'm sorry. Silence.
Did you ever think of us, when you were down there?
Down there? That makes it sound so pretty and nice. "Down there" was a prison.
She shows anger for the first time.
Do you know what a prison is? Hours and days in prison? Years in prison? The outside world leaving you behind. Life leaving you behind. Everything leaving you behind.
Awkward silence.
(calmer) Is she still living at Beaufans?

Mummy? No. I had to find a home for her. She lived with us, at first, then two years after Dad died, she began to lose her memory. Now, she doesn't recognize anyone. When I go to see her, she thinks I'm a nurse or a neighbour or something.
They never spoke about me?
Lea shakes her head.
What did they tell other people?
Anybody who knew the family knew better than to mention it. Anybody else thought I was their only daughter.
They thought that or they were told that?
They were told that?
Who told them? Mum and Dad?
Yes.
And you?
Lea doesn't answer. Tears well in her eyes.
They filled my head with it. Beat.
Juliette glances round the bar. Changes the subject.
(lighter) I slept with a guy last week.
You did what?
(smiles) I slept with a guy.
Lea stops crying. Begins to laugh.
Who? Where?
A guy I met in a cafe. We got a room in a hotel.
Just like that?
Just like that.
They both burst out laughing.
INT. SPARE ROOM - DAY

In a spare room, piled high with boxes, Juliette and Little Lili sit side by side at an old piano. Emelia sits on the floor. Juliette teaches Little Lili a song (A la Claire Fontaine). The piano needs tuning.
No, look! G, G, B, B, A, B, G, G, G, B, B, A, B... Try again. Little Lili plunks out the tune.
That's better! Spread your fingers more, like that... Good. And hit the keys harder.
It's not easy, Auntie!
Stop! G, G, B, B, A! You have to change note.
I'll never get it right.
Of course, you will! I used to play this with your Mummy, you know.
With Mummy?
Sure.
Mummy played the piano? I don't believe you.
She used to be very good. This tune was one of our favourites.
Because it's called Fontaine like you?
Juliette smiles and nods.
I never saw Mummy play! She won't let me play piano. She wants me to learn the flute. If she sees us in here, she'll be so angry!
No, she won't. Don't worry.
INT. OFFICE, HOSPITAL - DAY
A WOMAN sits behind a desk. Juliette sits opposite her. The Woman finishes reading a file and closes it.

You have all the qualifications for the job. But in this instance, even if it's just a secretary's job, I have to refer it to the director of the hospital who will make the final decision. If he approves it, nobody here must ever know that you were a doctor. And especially that you... You know what I mean.
Juliette is about to say something.
Don't thank me, I'm not doing it for you.
INT. LIVING ROOM - DAY

Reclining on the couch, with books and notes all around her, Lea prepares a lecture. Luc enters with a mug of tea and puts it on the coffee table. Lea thanks him without looking up. Luc goes over to the window. Outside, Juliette walks in the garden. Little Lili is also out there, running all over the place, but Juliette doesn't pay her any attention.
You wouldn't guess, looking at her.
(miles away) What?
She looks totally normal.
Lea perks up suddenly, glances out the window, then at Luc.
Stop it!
What? Maybe you'd prefer to forget what she did, but I can't!
Leave her alone!
INT. SWIMMING POOL - DAY
A big, oval swimming pool. Juliette and Lea float next to each other, arms and legs outstretched.
What did you want to do?

Me? I couldn't have become a doctor like you. They would have freaked, but there was never any chance of that. I was always more attracted to literature and languages. The day I told them, I could see the relief on their faces. (beat) The water's great, isn't it?
Juliette smiles and nods.
Hello there, Lea!
The camera pulls back to reveal MONSIEUR LUCIEN, a paunchy 65 -yearold, with a Clark Gable moustache, gold chain and signe t rings, and a spray-on tan.
Hi, Monsieur Lucien. How are you?
Very well. Aren't you going to introduce me?
Of course. This is my sister,
(to Juliette) Monsieur Lucien's a regular here.
Your sister? I see. Hello!
That's right, my sister. She's off
limits, Monsieur Lucien! He chuckles and walks on.
He comes down here to try to chat up women. He'll hit on anyone, young or old.
Does he ever get anywhere?
You'd be surprised!
Lea glances at Juliette as she floats in the water, eyes closed. She reaches out her hand and brushes her fingers over a small scar on Juliette's thigh.

I was running to you for a hug. I jumped up and you tripped over a branch. I can still see the blood pouring from the wound and your face going deathly white... I thought you were going to die, all because of me.
INT. KITCHEN - DAY
Luc sits with his two daughters. He hands Emelia a yoghurt, then goes back to reading the sports pages of the paper. Little Lili is on Papy Paul's lap, also eating yoghurt.
When's Mummy coming home?
I don't know. Soon.
And Auntie?
I've no idea.
Are they together?
I haven't a clue. Eat up! Beat.
Doesn't Auntie Juliette have a job?
Why'd you ask?
No reason. I just thought she must have a job because she's pretty old now.
Luc has his nose in the paper.
Well? What's her job?
You ask her, smarty-pants. Can't you see I'm trying to do some work?
INT. JULIETTE'S ROOM - EVENING
In the darkness, Juliette watches the rain falling on the back garden. There are papers scattered on her bed, and what looks to be a photograph.
INT. SOCIAL WORKER'S OFFICE - DAY
Well, that just about covers everything. Come back and see me in a few weeks. Meanwhile, I'll call the hospital to see what's happening. It would be the perfect job for you. I'll be in touch.
She walks to the door with Juliette. Takes out a cigarette. Offers one to Juliette as they go out into the hallway.
No, thanks.
I was wondering... I read your file and the notes on your trial... You never said a word throughout the investigation and the trial. Why? No answer.
You even refused to talk to the psychologists. Their report runs to two lines. Something about a lethal injection "foreshadowing the choice of
silence". I don't really get that.
(furious) And you think I'm going to talk to you now?

EXT. STREET - EVENING
Lea and Luc walk arm in arm down the road.
It's been ages since you dropped by the lab to pick me up.
Did you miss it?
Yeah, I like it when you surprise me.
How about a movie and then dinner?
Sure.
Is there anything you really want to see? There's a Kurosawa retrospective at the Cameo.
No way! Japanese films always put me to sleep.
Well, they're probably still showing The Shop Around The Corner.
Good idea. I haven't seen that in so long! But that means we'll be back really late, if you want to go for dinner afterwards.
It'll be fine. Beat.
Are you sure Katrina doesn't mind?
What's Katrina got to do with anything?

No, I asked Juliette.
You asked your sister to look after our daughters? Have you lost your mind? She killed her kid, remember! That's insane!
EXT/INT. LUC & LEA'S HOUSE - EVENING
Luc screeches to a halt outside the house. He leaps out of the car, races up the path and into the house.
(heart pounding) Lili? Emelia? Lili!!!
Juliette rushes out of the living room with a book in her hand.
(barks) Where are my daughters? Where are they?
Upstairs. They're asleep. We had dinner early and they were tired, so...
Before she can finish, Luc dashes upstairs. Juliette is stunned. Lea arrives.
You're back already? What's wrong with Luc?

INT. FINE ARTS MUSEUM - DAY

Juliette strolls through the empty museum. She stops in her tracks when she comes to a large ultra -realistic painting of a woman in mourning abou t to throw herself into a grave at the bottom of which lies a coffin. Other women hold her back. Men in dark suits and top hats stand in the background.

Juliette is startled.
You startled me.
Hi. (beat) Impressive, isn't it?
Who painted it?
Emile Friant. Famous when he was alive, totally forgotten since his death in the 1930s. I'll show you another of his that is my favourite. Come on...
They walk into the next room. Michel stops at a small painting showing a young woman's face against a snowy landscape.

The first time, I saw it, it blew me away. She looks exactly like a girl I was in love with in my early 20s. I was crazy about her. She didn't even see me, though. This is my reveng e. She's imprisoned in a frame and I can look at her whenever I want. She can't say a thing.
EXT. SIDEWALK CAFE - DAY
Juliette and Michel are having lunch.
Lea is amazing. She's one of the best specialists in France in her field. But she doesn't have the killer instinct. In our work, you have to trample over people to get ahead.
Do you have that killer instinct?
Do I look like I do?

No. I'm not into that anymore.

Yes, I had an interview for a secretary's job at the hospital.
Secretary? That's what you do?
Yes. I mean, no. It's complicated.
(smiles) In that case, I'll shut up. (raises his glass) To you!
To the girl in the museum!
Christ, no!

INT/EXT. CAR/RETIREMENT HOME - DAY
Lea parks her car.
Well?
No, I'd rather not. I'll wait here for you.
Lea grabs a bouquet of flowers off the backseat.
I won't be long.
She gets out. Sitting in the car, Juliette glances up at the windows of the
building that Lea entered.

Eventually, she gets out of the car, paces up and down the pavement, stops, leans against the car. In the garden of the retirement home, old people sit quietly on benches. Suddenly, Lea appears at one of the windows, opening the curtains. An old lady is standing next to her with her back to the window. ANGLE on Juliette. Her eyes fill with tears.
INT. CAFE - DAY
Juliette enters. Glances round the cafe. The Inspector gets up and waves to her. Juliette goes over. They sit down.
You don't mind that I suggested we meet here? I'm sick of my office.
No, I like cafes. It's one of the things I missed most when I was in prison. The noise, the atmosphere, the smoke and the buzz of conversations.
The Inspector notices she has wet hair.
Is it raining?
No, I've been swimming.
You swim? Me, too, but not in swimming pools. I told you about the Orinoco, didn't I?
Yes, the first time we met.
It's a huge, huge river. Over 1,500 miles long. It never ends. And it's powerful. Rapids, waterfalls, flood plains...
You've seen it for real?

Not yet. It's a project of mine. We'll see. It's not easy. I have a young daughter. She lives with her mother and I don't see her often. Her mother moved away. And my daughter has moved away from me, too. You know, the Orinoco is a real mystery. Several expeditions have set out to find its source and none of them has ever really succeeded. They found little streams, but not the real source. It's fascinating, isn't it? We know everything nowadays, but we can't find the source of a river. (beat) I signed and approved the papers.
Sorry?
For the job at the hospital. They asked for my opinion.
Thanks.
No problem. (the waiter arrives) What will you have?
INT. KITCHEN - EVENING
The family dinner is over. Papy Paul leaves the table. Juliette begins washing up. Little Lili is finishing her dessert. Lea isn't there.
Lili, when you finish, brush your teeth and straight to bed. Don't wake your sister.
When's Mummy coming home?
After her meeting.
Late?
I expect so. Go on, I'll come up and give you a kiss.
Little Lili hurries out. Luc and Juliette are alone. Luc seems ill at ease. Juliette keeps on washing up.
How did it go at the bank?
They let me open an account, but they won't give me a chequebook until I get a job.
Any news from the hospital?
Not a word. Beat.
Don't worry, Luc, I won't bother you much longer. I'll find something else.
(feebly) I never meant for you to think -
I want a story!
(gratefully) Coming right up, sweetheart.
He exits. Juliette wipes the table. Luc reappears, displeased.
She wants you to read her a story.
INT. LITTLE LILI'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Juliette sits on the bed, reading. Little Lili is fast asleep.

"The old man smiled and for a Moment, it seemed to Baji that his host was a boy of his own age, or a young girl, or an infant. In the sky, the silver stars twinkled and the moon rose, as full as a woman's tummy..." Juliette realizes that Little Lili is asleep. She quietly closes the book. And gazes at the child. Impassively. Eerily. Eventually, she leans forward and kisses Little Lili with real emotion for the first time. In the doorway, Lea watches. Juliette turns round and sees her. Lea smiles at her sister.
EXT. OUTSIDE LEA'S HOUSE - DAY
Luc loads up the car. The kids are already strapped in the backseat. Lea brings out sleeping bags, food and plates. Juliette helps them. They all seem excited to be going away.
Did you bring a sweater? It can get cold out there.
Right, thanks.
INT. HOUSE - DAY
Juliette comes downstairs with a sweater in one hand. Before leaving, she stops by Papy Paul's room. He is sitting in a chair, reading as ever.
Will you be okay? You have everyth ing you need? (beat) We'll be back tomorrow. Goodbye!

The family sings happily as they drive along.
EXT/INT. FARMHOUSE - DAY
The car pulls up outside a rundown farmhouse.
The duck pond.

Adults and children playing. More friends arrive. In all, there are over fifteen adults, including Michel, and Samir and his wife. Children bouncing around in the bedrooms. Women busy in the kitchen. Men opening bottles of wine. Playing soccer. The table set for dinner. MONTAGE ends.
INT. FARMHOUSE - NIGHT

An open fire blazes in the hearth. Children are fast asleep, curled up on the floor or in chairs. In one corner, the older children play a game. Around the table, the adults have flushed faces. Empty bottles litter the table. A joint goes round. Everybody's laughing and joking. One guy (GERARD) looks particularly drunk.
I don't have to like Rohmer! It's a free country.
I don't see how you can teach literature and not understand Rohmer's movies.
Lea didn't say she doesn't underst and them. She said she doesn't like them. There's a subtle difference.
I can't believe what I'm hearing! I can't help wondering how you explain Racine to your students!
That's irrelevant. What's Racine got to do with anything?
Rohmer is the Racine of the 20 th century, but if you guys are too stupid to see it...
That's totally unfair.
(hands Gerard the joint) Here, have some of this and chill out. Next, you'll be telling us that Stallone is Shakespeare.
Screw you! You don't care about anything except soccer. What about you, Juliette? What do you think?
Her face cupped in her hands, Juliette smiles and shrugs.
Juliette doesn't think anything.
Juliette watches and judges. But who
Cut it out! You're a pain in the ass. Go get some fresh air!
Gerard raps his knife against his glass.
Ladies and gentlemen, pray silence! I would humbly like to suggest we play a little game. The winner will receive my deepest consideration...
You can shove your consideration!
And a kiss from Juliette.
Gerard, you can be such an asshole!
The game is to find the reason why Lea hid her delightful sister from us for the last two hundred years. Where was Juliette? What was Juliette doing?
Knock it off!
Was Juliette on the other side of the world? Was she not speaking to Lea? Was Juliette...
Cut it out, Gerard.
...hidden somewhere in Switzerland? In a convent? Was Juliette...
You're getting on everybody's nerves!
... a lion tamer in a circus, a Mossad spy, an amnesic? Answer us, Juliette! Answer us!
Shut the fuck up, Gerard!
Be quiet, or I'll belt you!
Let Juliette speak! For once, I have a heroine from a novel in front of me, I want to hear her tell me...
That you're an asshole!
The truth! Juliette disappears.
Juliette reappears. Juliette?
MAN #1 Go to bed!
Not before I know where Juliette has been. The beautiful Juliette! Juliette! Juliette!
He gazes at her. Gradually, everybody else falls silent.
(deadpan) I was in prison. Fifteen years. I killed my son. Silence.
Suddenly, Gerard bursts out laughing. Everybody else joins in, except Lea, Luc and Michel.
That'll teach you, Gerard! WOMAN #1 Well done, Juliette!
What do you have to say now, loudmouth?
Okay, she wins. I give up.
The atmosphere lightens again. Juliette discreetly ups and leaves. Lea and Luc glance at each other.
EXT. LAKESIDE - NIGHT
Juliette stands by the lake. It's a very dark night. O.S., laughter filters from the farmhouse. Then, footsteps. Michel draws level with Juliette. Puts a hand on her shoulder. She shrugs it off.
Sorry about him. He's an ass when he's drunk, but he's a good guy. Beat.

They all think you were joking. I... (pause) I'm sure you weren't. Before I got my job at the university, I taught in prisons for ten years. I rarely talk about it... I used to go in three times a week. And I'd come out three times a week. That changed my whole life, my whole way of looking at things, people, the sky, passing time, coming and going, being in the street, everything. I stopped being so certain of things, making judgments the way people always do. Everybody I met behind bars, it seemed like they were just like me. They could have been me and I could have been them. It's a fine line sometimes.

Juliette is in the passenger seat.

Yes. Thanks for the ride.

Don't... Please... I'm still... a long way away.

Lea leads a discussion with a dozen students. She's tense, irritable. The STUDENT she is debating with is very calm and measured. As their argument develops, the other students glance at each other with raised eyebrows.
That's only true of Raskolnikov! You can't extend the notion of redemptive guilt to humanity in general and suggest that each murder contains its own redemption.
Yet the aim of the novelist is to reconstitute the world. Dostoyevsky is no exception.

In the case of this novel, you know full well that the narration is impersonal and, moreover, incomplete, as if the author refused to put forward a single worldview because he knows that there are numerous interpretations and motivations. And numerous shades of truth.

But the first draft is in the first person. One can deduce, therefore, that the initial project was indeed to portray a single soul that might give the reader an intimate yet universal insight into murder and murderers. Lea is really wound up. Her students are shocked.
You don't know what you're talking about! What do you know about murder and murderers! STUDENT I...

And what did Dostoyevsky know about it deep down? Well? Nothing! Nada! Zilch! Even masterpieces are no more than hypotheses, simplistic extrapolations that are nothing compared to true life. Nothing. Got that? If you stopped thinking a book is the last word on something, you wouldn't spout so much bullshit!
Stunned silence. Lea pulls herself together.
I... I am terribly sorry. I apologize. I don't know what...
She grabs her notes and hurries out.
INT. FACULTY BUILDING - DAY
Lea rushes down a hallway. When she reaches her office, she finds Bamakale sitting on a bench outside. He is impeccably dressed as always. As soon as he sees her, he jumps to his feet.
Good morning, I wondered if I -

INT. LEA'S OFFICE - DAY
Holding her head in her hands, Lea sobs.
INT. TYPING POOL, HOSPITAL - DAY
Juliette follows the woman who interviewed her for the job earlier. She introduces Juliette to a couple of other secretaries, typing away at their computers. They come to an empty desk.

This is your desk. Your job is essentially to type up medical reports and perhaps help out your colleagues from time to time. The trial period is three weeks. At the end of that period, the director will inform you of his decision. I'll let you get settled in.

Lea arrives home. Dumps grocery bags in the hall. Listens. O.S., on the piano, the tune Juliette taught Little Lili in an earlier scene. It clearly perturbs Lea. She hurries upstairs to the spare room where Little Lili is playing with Juliette next to her. Emelia sits on the floor. Papy Paul is in one corner, smiling. Little Lili stops and runs over to hug her mother.
Mummy! Come and play! Play, Mummy! Auntie said you used to be very good!

Lea hesitates. Juliette shuffles over on the piano stool, and nods to her to sit down. Lea still hesitates. Then goes over and sits at the piano. Each movement seems deeply thought -out. She gazes at the piano like a long -lost friend. Then her sister. They begin playing together. Juliette starts to sing.
A la claire fontaine m'en allant
J'ai trouve l'eau si claire Que je m'y suis baignee... (At the fountain as I was walking by The water was so clear That I want for a swim...)

Il y a longtemps que je t'aime
(I've loved you so long I can never forget you...) J'ai trouve l'eau si claire Que je m'y suis baignee...

(I've loved you so long I can never forget you...)

The children applaud. Papy Paul looks delighted. The girls hug their mother and kiss her. Lea and Juliette glance at each other, deeply moved. Juliette runs her hand down Lea's arm.
INT. TYPING POOL, HOSPITAL - DAY
A headset in her ears, Juliette typ es up a report. Three young male DOCTORS walk over. One of them lobs a tape onto Juliette's desk.
Type that and send it over to Cardiology.
Juliette removes her headset.
Sorry?
(irritably) I said, type that now and send it over to Cardio.

Fine.

(mutters to his colleagues) They're hiring them older than before and more stupid than ever!
INT. CAFE - DAY
The Inspector and Juliette sit at a table.
At college.
He was a doctor, too?
No, he dropped out after two years. He got a job supervising a team of health visitors.
When did you last see him?
At the trial. He testified against me.

Like my wife. Ever since, she's never... She sends my daughter here by plane or train with a label round her neck like a parcel. (beat) I could still describe exactly how she was dressed the first time we met. (beat) How's work?
Fine.
Nobody giving you any trouble?
No...
By the way, I think I'll soon be... You remember my Orinoco project... After all this time, it's much clearer in my mind now. Deep down, there's
nothing and nobody to make it worth me
INT. KITCHEN - EVENING

Juliette sits with Emelia on her lap, flicking through a picture book, pointing to a picture and saying the word out loud several times. Emelia tries to repeat the words. Suddenly, there's a loud crash upstairs, followed by screams.
INT. LEA & LUC'S BEDROOM - EVENING
Luc writhes in pain on the floor, clutching his shoulder. Nearby, scattered tools and a stepladder lying on its side.
You idiot!
Will you shut up? It hurts.
Little Lili pokes her head round the door.
Daddy! What happened?
Juliette rushes in, with Emelia in her arms. She puts Emelia down and kneels next to Luc. All her doctor's reflexes come flooding back.

Daddy!

INT. SWIMMING POOL - DAY
Juliette and Lea hold onto the side, kicking their legs.

Michel, for example, is the kind of

No... I don't think so. Actually, I don't know. He's actually pretty secretive. (beat) I don't believe it! Check that out!
Look who Monsieur Lucien has on his arm. Paunchy Monsieur Lucien struts along in the company of a beautiful 25-year-old. He winks at Juliette and Lea.

Lea places a heavy box on the desk, under Juliette's nose.

It's called teamwork. Even if you each have your individual tasks to do, there has to be unity, team spirit. We're not machines. Several of your co-workers say you are cold and distant. You never talk to anyone apparently. It's disappointing. Very disappointing.
Does that mean I'm out of a job?

Of course not! I didn't say that, did I? Just don't forget you're on trial. Make an effort. That's all I'm saying. Try to be less... withdrawn. Open up a bit. (beat) Of course, I understand that it's not... I mean, I understand but...
What do you understand?
The Director has no answer.
INT. EMELIA & LITTLE LILI'S ROOMS - NIGHT
Lea tucks Emelia in. She is already asleep. Lea kisses her, turns on a nightlight and slips out. She goes into Little Lili's room and finds her daughter reading.
Okay, Little Lili, lights out now.
Lea closes the book, puts it on the nightstand, kisses her daughter and is about to turn out the light...
No, please! Leave it on!
Alright, sweetheart. Goodnight.
Mummy, I want to ask you something.
Lea stops and turns round.

segment-speaker: LILI When you and Auntie Juliette were
segment-text: little, did you spend a lot of time together?
Quite a lot, yes.
But Auntie Juliette was much ol der than you.
Lea nods.
So she protected you?
Against absolutely everything.
So why did she stop one day?
What do you mean?
Why'd she go away for so long and leave you all alone?
Because I wasn't a little girl anymore. Sleep now.
She kisses Little Lili and turns to leave.
We all need protecting, don't we, though? Even when we're grown up.
(laughs) That's what your Dad's for.
(laughs too) I didn't think of that.
Goodnight.
You know, Mummy, I really like Aunti e Juliette.
Me, too, sweetheart.
You can tell she's your sister because she's really kind, like you. It's a shame she never had children, though.
What makes you say that?
I'd have liked to have some cousins.
Go to sleep.
EXT. STREETS - NIGHT
Juliette and Michel come out of a cinema.
Do you want to go for a drink?
It's a warm night. Let's walk.
They walk along in silence, apparently happy together, here, now.

For two years, the only walking I got to do was in a tiny triangular courtyard with big, high walls... I counted my steps, every step I took. And kept on counting later when I was at Centrale. The guards called me The Walker. But the other inmates called me Nobody.
Michel looks quizzically.
Short for Nobody's Home.
There's a Giono novel - I'm pretty sure it's his last - that has a great female character, who's withdrawn into silence. She calls herself Nobody. The
main character falls madly in love
Do you see the world through books you've read?
Books have been a big help to me. Sometimes, they are better friends than people, don't you find?
He glances at her as they walk along. Eventually, she turns to look at him, too.
INT. KITCHEN - DAY
Breakfast. Everybody's in the kitchen. Luc is about to leave for work. He steals a slice of toast from Little Lili's plate.
Hey! That's mine!
I know but I'm terribly late. I gotta run. Bye, everybody! And don't wait up for me, I have a meeting that will finish late.
You have a meeting? So do I. I won't be home either. I told you about it.
What about us?
Katrina will look after you. Mummy's going to call her.
You're out of luck, sunshine. Katrina's away wreaking destruction
and havoc at her mother-in-law's.
Papy Paul can look after us. Can't you, Papy!
Luc and Lea share a glance. He hesitates. Lea leaves him on his own.
Maybe... Juliette could look after you. If she doesn't mind, of course. (to Juliette) But maybe you have other plans?
No, it'd be a pleasure.
Yes! Thank you, Auntie!
INT. MICHEL'S OFFICE, FACULTY BLDG - DAY
Michel types his students' marks out of twenty. Lea dictates.
Laure Sourdillon, 10. Eric Suffren, 3. Anne Sulkovic, 8. Laurent Taskon, 11. Frederic Taskon, 6. Cecile Tramelle, 18. No way! Did she bribe you?
I'm using a new system of bonus points. Female? Plus 3. Belly showing? Plus 2. Thong showing? Plus 5. No bra? Plus 4. Belly button pierced? Plus 1.
You slimeball! I'll tell Juliette on you. She was beginning to like you.
She told you that?
Perhaps.
Seriously, what did she say?
Look at you! You're like you're 15 years old! You are just so cute at times! (beat)
Frederic Trousseau, 8. Ludivine
C'mon, Lea, tell me!
(shakes her head, smiles) Sophie Uzes, 3. Dogun Uzbek, 14. Louis
Valdenaire, 7. Ludovic Vogel, 11...
INT. LEA'S HOUSE - NIGHT
Juliette tiptoes out of Little Lili's room. Closes the door and puts a book of fairy tale s down on a chair. She goes downstairs. Sees a light under Papy Paul's door. Knocks gently and enters.
May I?

Papy Paul smiles at her. Juliette sits opposite him and smiles back. Clearly, she finds his presence soothing. Papy Paul goes back to his book. Juliette sits, saying nothing. From time to time, he looks up and smiles at her. O.S., a key in the front door. Juliette gets up and says goodnight. In the hall, Lea is home. The two sisters talk in whispers.
How did it go? Are they asleep?
Like logs.
I'm going to go to bed, too. I'm
exhausted. Is Luc home?
Not yet.
You're still coming tomorrow? Beat.
Juliette nods.
INT. RETIREMENT HOME - DAY

Juliette and Lea walk down a hallway, past old folks in blue towelling robes. Lea carries a bouquet of flowers. They reach a door. Lea knocks and enters. Juliette hovers on the threshold. In the room, their MOTHER spins round when she hears the door open.
Hi, Mummy!
(strong English accent) I already told you I don't need anything!
It's me, Mum.
Who are you? I don't know you. If you don't leave this minute, I'll call for help!
Mum, it's me, Lea. Your daughter.
Oh, I know who you are. I told you before, I don't need anything. I have at least three vacuum cleaners already, why would I need another one? At my age.
I brought you some flowers.
Don't think you'll get round me like that!
Juliette has ventured a few feet inside the door.
(re. Juliette) Is she with you?
Mum...
Stop calling me that! It's very annoying! Don't just stand there, fetch a vase! Everything I have gets stolen here.
Lea glances at Juliette, then leaves the room. Their mother sits in a chair.
It's a funny job you do. Pestering people all day. Can't you take your junk somewhere else?
She leans forward, takes her head in both hands and shakes it. Stops. Looks up and stares straight at Juliette. Her face is unrecognizable. Her lips quiver.
(gently, in English) My darling Juliette... Are you back from school already? Come and give me a big kiss! Come and kiss your Mummy!
Juliette freezes.
Come on, sweety. What's wrong? Come on... I am so happy to see you! I am so happy!
The old lady holds her hand out. Juliette eventually takes hold of it. Her mother pulls her towards her. Juliette is caught between astonishment, the urge to run and great emotion.
I'm all alone here, you know. They locked me up! I'm all, all alone. Take me home with you. Take me home, please.
She sobs. Juliette strokes her hair and forehead. Just then, Lea returns. She stops in her tracks. Eventually, her mother looks up. She is transformed again.
(in French) That's quite enough! (notices Juliette standing beside her) What are you doing, pressing up against me? You've got a nerve! Get out of here now, both of you! Go on! Out!
EXT. HOSPITAL/STREET S - DAY
Juliette comes out of the hospital. She catches a bus into town. Stands the whole way. It's full. She walks down a busy street in the town centre. She dials a number on a public phone. MONTAGE ends.
Michel? Hi, it's Juliette.
Hi, there! How are you doing?
Fine. I'm in town. I thought maybe we could meet up?
I'm really sorry. I have a meeting starting in half an hour and...
After your meeting maybe... I can go for a walk in the meantime.
It'll probably drag on. And I promised a friend I'd meet him afterwards. Another time, okay?
(colder) Okay. Sorry to bother you. Goodbye.
Goodbye, Juliette.
She hangs up, her disappointment accentuated by the fact that Michel was clearly not telling her something.
INT. BAR - EVENING

A fairly cramped space. Juliette stands at the bar, a glass of red wine in front of her. Sips at it. Lined up at the bar, other customers sip their beer or Ricard in much the same way, lost in their drink or their thoughts. Nobody talks. Nobody pays any attention to anyone else. In the background, the radio plays. The Bar Owner does his accounts. A banal, but odd glimpse of daily human existence.
EXT/INT. LEA'S HOUSE - NIGHT
Juliette pushes the gate open and walks up to the house. Inside the house, silence. No lights are on.
Hello? Anyone home?

She knocks on Papy Paul's door. No answer. She glances in: his chair is empty. Back in the hallway, Juliette calls out again. No answer. She heads into the living room. Lea, Luc, the girls, Papy Paul, Samir, his (very pregnant) wife and Michel leap up and start singing.
Happy birthday to you! Happy birthday to you! Happy birthday, dear Juliette! Happy birthday to you!
They cheer and applaud. The two girls carry over a huge gift -wrapped present. Juliette tries to say something, but the words catch in her throat . Her eyes fill with tears. She laughs.
INT. MICHEL'S APARTMENT - DAY
Michel is stretched out on the couch. Juliette sits on the floor, her head resting on Michel's thigh.

The worst thing, maybe, was the end, the last few weeks before it was ove r and after they told me I was being released. I started having nightmares. Always the same one, in fact. I had my case packed. I stepped outside and found myself in the middle of nowhere. There was nothing and nobody there. Just emptiness. One day, they told me I had a visitor. I thought it was a mistake. It was the first time ever. There was a young woman waiting to see me. She smiled.

I listened to her without saying a word. She promised she'd come again. That night, I promised myself I'd refuse to see her. She belonged to a part of my life that had died. Then, just before I went to sleep, I thought back to when she was little. Lala, we used to call her. The gaps in her teeth, her smile, her fringe that would never stay in the same place... And her hand in mine. It was that little girl that made me decide to come back.
INT. LOCKER ROOM, SWIMMING POOL - DAY
Lea and Juliette stand side by side, drying their hair in the mirror.
Luc wants to take a trip to the mountains. "Top up the red blood cells," as he puts it. Being fit for his stupid soccer games is all that matters to him.
Lea pauses and gazes at Juliette's face in the mirror. They stare at each other's reflection as if seeking the answer to a much deeper mystery.
When I was little, I so wanted to be like you.
And now? Beat.
They go back to drying their hair.
You know what I thought about last
night? The Green House at Hossegor.
I went back once more after that.
Lea glances quizzically at her sister, but a bunch of women passing noisily by, O.S., stop her pressing the issue. The women's voices and laughter eventually fade.
I thought it had been knocked down.
Fifteen years ago, it was still standing.
Lea is intrigued now.
I took Pierre there.
Lea gazes at Juliette's reflection, but doesn't dare ask.
INT. LEA'S HOUSE - EVENING
Little Lili watches admiringly as Juliette does her make -up in her
room.
Lea peeks round the door. She looks a million dollars. They all go downstairs where Luc has Emelia in his arms.
You look very beautiful, Auntie!
Do you think so?
She picks up two pairs of earrings.
These ones or these ones?
Mmmm... The red ones! After the restaurant, which nightclub are you going to?
That's for us to know and you to find out. (to Juliette) Are you ready?
Absolutely!
Can you dance, Auntie?
Of course. I'll teach you if you want.
Yes! I'd love to come with you.
You're still a bit on the young side, cupcake.
I'm not a cupcake and I'm eight years old!
(chants) Eight years old! Eight years old!
Papy Paul pokes his head out of his room, smiling.
(to the girls) See you, chickadees! Be good. Don't give your father a hard time. He looks exhausted already.
She gives them each a kiss. Luc whispers in her ear.
Don't come home too late, will you?
You can bank on it, sweety!
She gives him one last kiss and a sly wink.
INT. NIGHTCLUB - NIGHT

Juliette and Lea are caught in the throng in the club entrance. Chatter. Laughter. Throbbing music. Lea can't stop grinning as they shuffle towards the dance floor. Behind her, as the music gets louder, Juliette looks increasingly uncomfortable. Lea doesn't notice a thing. The camera closes in on her. After a few seconds, she glances round. Juliette has disappeared.
EXT. PARKING LOT, NIGHTCLUB - NIGHT

Lea rushes out of the club, calling out Juliette's name. Finally, she hears sobbing coming fro m around a corner. Juliette is squatting on the ground, crying her eyes out. Lea hunkers down and takes her in her arms. Juliette clings to Lea like a life buoy.
INT. CAR - NIGHT Lea drives.
Juliette sits in a ball in the passenger seat. Haggard, tear streaked cheeks. Lea eventually breaks the silence.
You can tell me anything. You can talk to me...
No reaction.
I'm not other people.
Juliette glances at her sister.
Juliette, I'm not other people.
INT. POLICE STATION - DAY
Juliette sits on the same bench in the waiting room as in a previous scene. Two people sit nearby. A YOUNG INSPECTOR arrives.
(reads from a card) Juliette Fontaine?
Yes.
Follow me.

She follows him down the hallway. He doesn't say a word. He shows her into the same office as before. But it doesn't look the same. The Orinoco poster has been replaced by an official National Police Force poster. The young Inspector sits at the desk and opens a file. Juliette hesitates and decides t o stay standing. He talks without taking his eyes off the file.
My name is Segral. I'm your new probation officer, replacing Inspector
(brightly) So he finally did it? He went to see the Orinoco?
The Inspector tears his eyes from the file and stares at Juliette as if she's totally nuts.
If going to see the Orinoco means blowing your brains out, yes, he finally did it. Ten days ago.
Juliette gasps and grabs the back of the chair in front of her.
EXT. OUTSIDE LEA'S HOUSE - DAY
Juliette walks home. Outside the house, Lea is about to get into the car. The girls and Luc are already in their seats.
Juliette! Hurry! We're going to the clinic! Kaisha gave birth last night. A little girl. We're going to see her .
Juliette shakes her head.
Come on, you can have a nap later if you're tired. The girls are so
Forget it.
Juliette heads up the path to the house. Lea gazes at her blankly.
INT. JULIETTE'S BEDR OOM - DAY
Juliette lies on her bed, clasping a photo and a sheet of paper to her chest.
INT. TYPING POOL - DAY
Juliette is hard at work.
The Director wants to see you. He asked me to tell you.
Juliette glances up anxiously.
Thanks... I'll be right there.
EXT. GARDEN, LEA'S HOUSE - DAY
On a table in the garden, Lea, Juliette and the two girls are making acacia blossom fritters. They all seem in good spirits. Papy Paul reclines in a deck chair, reading and smiling.
No, Little Lili, not like that! That's too much sugar. Two spoonfuls is enough, I said!
Emelia, you're getting flour everywhere!
Is that okay, Auntie?
That's perfect. Now, pour in the milk.
Do you think we'll have enough flowers?
Sure, and if we don't, we can pick some more.
Luc arrives home from work.
(surprised) Good grief, it's a party! (kisses Lea) What are we celebrating?
Juliette and Lea glance at each other and smile.
You tell him.
The hospital has given me a full -time contract.
Wow, that's great! That calls for a drink!
My impeccable sense of timing...
He comes round the corner, carrying four bottles of champagne.
Hello, everybody! Look who I brought with me - a whole tribe of starving Afghanis!
Everybody looks past him to see Samir, Kaisha and their new baby arriving. More cheers.
INT. KITCHEN - DAY
An apron tied round her waist, Juliette puts the last fritter on the serving dish that Little Lili is ready to take out. O.S., laughter and banter from the garden.
Careful you don't burn yourself. And don't drop it either.

Little Lili walks out as if the platter were a royal crown. Juliette watches her affectionately. Wipes her hands. Lea arrives. The sisters glance at each other with love and great affection. Juliette opens her mouth to speak but bites her tongue.
What?
Juliette smiles and shakes her head. She gazes at Lea once more.
Thank you.
INT. FINE ARTS MUSEUM - DAY

Juliette wanders round the museum, tightly framed by the camera. We see her face in close-up, with a blur of paintings behind her as she looks round. She stops at a painting of the Annunciation. The camera closes in on the face of the Angel Gabriel telling Mary the good news. Juliette seems to be in key with the painting.
Here, look at this one.

We pull back to see that Michel has been walking along with Juliette all this time, his arm round her waist. He points to another painting. Juliette looks, then averts her eyes rapidly. Michel doesn't notice. The camera pulls back still further, until we lose them in the vastness of the museum. An ordinary couple. Just like any other.
INT. JULIETTE'S BEDR OOM - DAY
Juliette puts away her freshly laundered clothes in the cupboard. The photo and sheet of paper are tucked be tween two sweaters. Juliette takes them out and kisses them.
(from downstairs) Juliette? It's 8:30 already! If you want me to give you a lift, it's right now!
Coming!

She is about to tuck the photo out of sight again, but thinks twice. She gazes at the cupboard, frowns, then turns round and gently puts the photo and sheet of paper down on the bed, in full view. At the door, Juliette glances round at the bed and heaves a sigh of relief, as if a huge weight has been lifted from her sh oulders.
INT. LIVING ROOM - DAY
Lea has pushed back the furniture to clean.
(loudly) What was it this time, Marie-Paule? A cat? The wind? The holy spirit?
I never said that at all, Lea. What I said was that Papy Paul stumbled and the lamp fell off. You can't blame me for that!
(to herself) Papy Paul! Sure. I believe you. (louder) When you've finished in the kitchen, come and give me a hand moving the table. I know I can't do it on my own.
Okay!
Emelia skitters into the living room, gurgling happily. She has something in her hand that we can't make out.
You've made a quick recovery, you little terror! If I'd known, I'd have taken you to creche.
Mummy! Mummy!
She holds out her hand.
What have you got there?
Lea reaches out and takes it. Freezes. It's a photo. Of a little boy, laughing. Lea immediately recognizes Pierre, Juliette's son. Emelia gurgles some more. Lea snaps out of her trance.
Where did you get this?
Auntie! Auntie!
Emelia, it's wrong to take other people's things. It's wrong! I forbid it! I forbid it!
Emelia bursts into tears.
INT. JULIETTE'S BEDR OOM - DAY

Lea enters her sister's bedroom and sees the cupboard door is open. A sheet of paper lies on the bed. A child's handwriting and hearts drawn on the page draw her eye. Lea picks up the paper. O.S., Emelia is still crying and calling for her "Mummy! Mummy!" Lea holds the photo in one hand and the sheet of paper in the other. The camera scans the handwriting as she reads: A garden in the rain Is peaceful and sad too Mummy, I feel the same
Your smile lights up the sky And makes me so happy

Your loving son, Pierre Lea stares at the page, deeply moved. She flips it over and her emotion gives way to surprise. The camera just gives us time to make out lines of figures and columns that look like the results of a number of tests. Lea is intrigued. She isn't sure what to do. Then, she leaves the room and goes to her study, where she scans the document.
INT. CAFE - DAY
From the outside looking in, we see Juliette sitting in the cafe. She looks out. Watches people passing by. She seems at peace with herself.
INT. SPARE ROOM - NIGHT
Little Lili and Juliette play the piano and sing happily. JULIETTE & LILI Sur la plus haute branche
Chante, rossignol, chante Toi, tu as le coeur gai (On the highest branch A nightingale sang Sing, nightingale, sing Your heart is full of joy)
Juliette gets up.
I have to go.
(keeps playing) No!
Juliette goes out into the hallway.
Mummy! Can you come and play with me?
Juliette joins Lea, who is tidying away clothes in Little Lili's dresser.
Is something wrong?
No, I'm fine.
Are you sure?
Lea stops what she's doing and gazes at her sister. O.S., Little Lili keeps playing.
INT. DOCTOR'S OFFICE - DAY
In a white coat, stethoscope round his neck, Samir sits reading the scanned document. Lea sits anxiously opposite him. Beat. Samir looks up and frowns.
Why did you tear the name off the top of the page? No answer.
It's Lili, isn't it?
(taken aback) No, not at all.
Do you swear?
I swear it isn't Little Lili, Samir. Honestly! You don't know the little... It's somebody who died. A long time ago.
So why all the mystery?
I can't explain now. Please, no questions. This is just very important for me. Very, very important.

Samir glances over the document again. Lea observes him closely, then looks away. Sees a framed photo on the desk. It shows Samir when he was younger with a young woman and two children aged about four and six. They are all smiling.

I'll have to consult a colleague whose field this is. I don't want to mislead you. All I can say for the moment is that it doesn't look very good. Not good at all. I'll call you as soon as I have more.

Lea smiles gratefully and gets up to go. Samir comes round to get the door for her. Suddenly, Lea gives him a big hug. She glances at the p hoto on the desk again. Samir seems surprised by this show of affection. Then, he notices what she's looking at.
(puts his hand over his heart) They're still here, you know. In the end, war is pretty weak. It can't destroy everything.
EXT. STREET - DAY Juliette waits outside an apartment building.
Lea runs to meet her. They kiss on the cheeks.
Sorry...
The woman from the agency gave me the keys. She'll meet us there.
INT. EMPTY APARTMENT - DAY
Juliette and Lea look round a charming old a partment. From separate rooms, they talk without being able to see each other.
Well?
What?
What do you think?
(distractedly) It's not bad.
You don't sound convinced.
No, it is. It gets plenty of light.
Juliette joins Lea in the next room. Beat.
I liked having you at home with us.
You've been acting strange for the last couple of days. What's wrong?
They look straight at each other. Lea is about to say something, when the front door opens. ESTATE AGENT (O.S.) Madame Fontaine?
Yes!
INT. LEA'S OFFICE, FACULTY BLDG - DAY
Lea sits at her desk facing Bamakale, elegantly dressed as always. Lea seems weary and bored by his story.
You have to understand, I feel like I have been passed from pillar to post for the last few months. I have been the victim of a terrible mistake. I can't sleep. I don't know where to turn...
The phone rings.
Lea picks up and motions to Bamakale to give her a minute.
Yes, Charly? ... Right, put him through. Thanks. (beat) Samir? ... No, it's fine. Go ahead.

Lea listens, brow furrowed. Gradually, her expression changes as if she has made an astonishing realization. When Samir has finished, she simply thanks him and hangs up. She seems to have completely forgotten ab out Bamakale. Eventually, he breaks the silence.
As I was saying, I have been treated very unfairly. This isn't the first time, the people of Africa have suffered an injustice. You are well aware...
Lea looks up, as if only just noticing his pres ence.
... that my people have been oppressed for centuries -
Mr. Bamakale, you really are beginning to piss me off with your complaints about your marks! You know that? You're pissing me off!
Juliette's voice fades in while the screen is still black.

I knew as soon as the first symptoms appeared. I hardly even read the results of the tests. Pierrot grabbed the letter and started writing a poem on it. When he gave it back, he was beaming with pride. My little boy.. . (beat) I saw him looking at me, so happy, so beautiful. He was my Pierrot. Then, I pictured the little corpse he was going to become.
INT. LIVING ROOM - DAY

Juliette stands by the window, looking out at the garden. A few paces behind her, Lea gazes at her sister. (As the scene develops, Lea will get closer to Juliette until she is pressed up against her sister. We sense they have cried a lot already. They are beyond tears. Their eyes are puffy and red-rimmed. They are reaching the conclusion of a long, painful discussion.)

I could picture him at every stage of the disease. I knew exactly how it would develop. The worst thing was that I knew he wouldn't just fall asleep and never wake up, like in a kiddies' story. Soon, he wouldn't be
able to move. He would suffer and
Why didn't you say...

I felt so alone. So alone. Anyway, there was nothing to say. Trying to explain it would be trying to excuse it. Death has no excuses. One way or another, I was guilty. I had brought a child into the world only to sentence him to death. (beat) You know, the most horrible prison... The worst prison is the death of you r child. There is never any release from that prison.
Lea holds Juliette in her arms. Juliette clings to her sister. Lea gives her a kiss. Silence.
Look...
Outside, in the garden, the sun comes out, even though it's still raining.
Isn't it beautiful?
O.S., the front door opens.
It's Michel! Is anyone home?
The sisters say nothing.
Lea? Juliette?
Juliette turns away from the garden, glances at Lea, smiles.
Juliette? Beat.
I'm here!
